metric_value: There might not be increasing budget market demand in the United States, but in other parts of the world such as Thailand or Malaysia, the so-called white-box market represents 40 percent of available sales.
notes: Kastner quote per Jay Lyman TechNewsWorld

metric_value: To compete with the very low-end systems, AMD needs a mature and inexpensive processor because cost is more important in that market.

Insight 64 analyst Dean McCarron frames AMD's split-branding strategy as clearly separating the mainstream segment where people are willing to pay more from the low-cost market where products are purchased on price.

AMD launched the Sempron CPU brand July 2004 (one month after this article's publication), placing Sempron in the low-end segment previously held by Athlon XP and freeing the Athlon brand to compete in mainstream/premium alongside Athlon 64.

AMD gradually retired the Sempron brand through 2013, replaced by Athlon low-end and later Ryzen 3; the Sempron-vs-Celeron low-end duopoly Kastner described ended as the overall low-end-desktop segment shrank.
